questions.question:
Consider the set (column) vectors defined by X = { x in R^3 | x1 + x2 + x3 = 0, where x = [x1, x2, x3]^T }. Which of the following is TRUE?

A. { [-1,0,1]^T, [1,-1,0]^T } is a basis for the subspace X.
B. { [-1,0,1]^T, [1,-1,0]^T } is a linearly independent set, but it does not span X and therefore is not a basis of X.
C. X is not a subspace of R^3.
D. None of the above.
questions.answer: A. { [-1,0,1]^T, [1,-1,0]^T } is a basis for the subspace X.

understand.plain: X is the set of all 3-dimensional column vectors whose three entries sum to zero. Any such vector satisfies one linear constraint, so X is a 2-dimensional subspace of R^3. The question asks whether a given pair of vectors is a basis (i.e. linearly independent and spanning) for X.

keywords.explain: A subset of a vector space that is itself a vector space: closed under addition and scalar multiplication, and contains the zero vector.
keywords.example: All vectors [x1,x2,x3]^T with x1+x2+x3=0 form a subspace because sums and scalar multiples of solutions are still solutions.

keywords.explain: A linearly independent set of vectors that spans the entire subspace. The number of basis vectors equals the dimension.
keywords.example: Two linearly independent vectors that lie in a 2-dimensional plane form a basis for that plane.

keywords.explain: The dimension of the solution space of Ax=0 equals n minus the rank of A. One equation in 3 unknowns gives a solution space of dimension 3-1=2.
keywords.example: x1+x2+x3=0 is one equation, so its solution space has dimension 2.

understand.visual_alt: X is the plane x1+x2+x3=0 through the origin in R^3, a 2-dimensional subspace.

given.aim: Determine which statement about the subspace X = { x in R^3 | x1+x2+x3=0 } and the set S = { [-1,0,1]^T, [1,-1,0]^T } is true.

terms.meaning: The plane through the origin in R^3 defined by x1+x2+x3=0.
terms.connects: X is a 2-dimensional subspace because one homogeneous linear equation in 3 unknowns leaves 2 free variables.

terms.meaning: A candidate basis consisting of two vectors that lie inside X.
terms.connects: If these two vectors are linearly independent, they form a basis for the 2-dimensional space X.

terms.meaning: Two vectors are linearly independent if neither is a scalar multiple of the other.
terms.example: [-1,0,1]^T is not a scalar multiple of [1,-1,0]^T because the second components (0 and -1) have ratio 0 while the first components (-1 and 1) have ratio -1.
terms.connects: Independence confirms the two vectors form a basis once we also verify they span X.

given.plan: 1. Verify both vectors lie in X. 2. Confirm X has dimension 2 (one constraint in R^3). 3. Check the two vectors are linearly independent. 4. Conclude they form a basis for X.

steps.title: Verify dimension of X
steps.apply: X is defined by one equation x1+x2+x3=0, so the coefficient matrix A = [1,1,1] has rank 1. Thus dim(X) = 3-1 = 2.
steps.note: X needs exactly 2 linearly independent vectors to form a basis.

steps.title: Check both vectors belong to X
steps.note: Any basis vector must first be a member of the subspace.

steps.title: Check linear independence
steps.apply: Suppose c1*[-1,0,1]^T + c2*[1,-1,0]^T = 0. Row 2 gives -c2=0, so c2=0. Row 1 then gives -c1=0, so c1=0. The vectors are linearly independent.
steps.note: Two linearly independent vectors in a 2-dimensional space automatically span that space.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Two linearly independent vectors lying in the 2-dimensional subspace X span X. Therefore S = { [-1,0,1]^T, [1,-1,0]^T } is a basis for X.

solution.result: A. { [-1,0,1]^T, [1,-1,0]^T } is a basis for the subspace X.

questions.formula_note: No standard registry formula was needed; the result follows from the rank-nullity theorem and the definition of a basis.

questions.question: If V1 and V2 are 4-dimensional subspaces of a 6-dimensional vector space V, then the smallest possible dimension of V1 ∩ V2 is _____.

understand.plain: The dimension formula for two subspaces states that dim(V1+V2) = dim(V1) + dim(V2) - dim(V1 ∩ V2). Since V1+V2 is itself a subspace of V, its dimension cannot exceed dim(V) = 6. This inequality constrains how small dim(V1 ∩ V2) can be.

keywords.explain: V1 ∩ V2 is always a subspace (the set of vectors belonging to both subspaces). Its dimension can be computed via the dimension formula.
keywords.example: Two planes through the origin in R^3 intersect in at least a line (dimension 1).

keywords.explain: dim(V1+V2) = dim(V1) + dim(V2) - dim(V1 ∩ V2), where V1+V2 is the sum (span of both subspaces).
keywords.example: If dim(V1)=3, dim(V2)=3, and dim(V)=4, then dim(V1 ∩ V2) >= 3+3-4=2.

keywords.explain: V1+V2 is contained in V, so dim(V1+V2) <= dim(V).
keywords.example: With dim(V)=6, the sum V1+V2 can have dimension at most 6.

understand.visual_alt: Two 4-dimensional subspaces inside a 6-dimensional space must overlap in at least a 2-dimensional subspace.

given.aim: Find the smallest possible dimension of V1 ∩ V2 given dim(V1)=4, dim(V2)=4, dim(V)=6.

terms.connects: Limits the maximum dimension of any subspace or their sum to 6.

terms.example: Each is spanned by 4 linearly independent vectors in V.
terms.connects: Their sum V1+V2 has dimension at most 6.

terms.meaning: The set of all vectors belonging to both V1 and V2.
terms.example: If V1 = span{e1,e2,e3,e4} and V2 = span{e3,e4,e5,e6}, then V1 ∩ V2 = span{e3,e4}, dimension 2.
terms.connects: Minimizing dim(V1 ∩ V2) means maximizing dim(V1+V2), subject to it being at most 6.

given.plan: Use the dimension formula: dim(V1 ∩ V2) = dim(V1) + dim(V2) - dim(V1+V2). Maximize dim(V1+V2) up to dim(V) = 6 to minimize dim(V1 ∩ V2).

steps.title: Apply the subspace dimension formula
steps.note: To minimize the intersection dimension, we want to maximize dim(V1+V2).

steps.title: Apply the upper bound on V1+V2
steps.apply: The maximum possible value of dim(V1+V2) is 6 (when V1+V2 = V). This is achievable: let V1 = span{e1,e2,e3,e4} and V2 = span{e3,e4,e5,e6} in R^6; then V1+V2 = R^6.

steps.title: Compute minimum intersection dimension
steps.apply: When dim(V1+V2) = 6, dim(V1 ∩ V2) = 8-6 = 2. With the example above, V1 ∩ V2 = span{e3,e4}, which indeed has dimension 2.
steps.note: The smallest possible dimension of the intersection is 2.

questions.formula_note: The key tool is the subspace dimension formula: dim(V1 ∩ V2) = dim(V1) + dim(V2) - dim(V1+V2).

questions.question:
Let u and v be two vectors in R^2 whose Euclidean norms satisfy |u| = 2|v|. What is the value of alpha such that w = u + alpha*v bisects the angle between u and v?

A. 2
B. 1/2
C. 1
D. -1/2

understand.plain: A vector w bisects the angle between u and v when the angle from u to w equals the angle from w to v. The standard result is that w = (u/|u|) + (v/|v|) — the sum of the unit vectors in the directions of u and v — bisects the angle between them. We must write this in the form u + alpha*v and find alpha.

keywords.explain: The vector that makes equal angles with both given vectors. It equals the sum of the two unit direction vectors.
keywords.example: If u = [1,0]^T and v = [0,1]^T, both unit vectors, then w = u+v = [1,1]^T bisects the 90-degree angle.

keywords.explain: A vector divided by its magnitude, giving a vector of length 1 pointing in the same direction.
keywords.example: The unit vector in the direction of [3,4]^T is [3/5, 4/5]^T.

keywords.explain: The length of a vector: |v| = sqrt(v1^2 + v2^2 + ... + vn^2).

understand.visual_alt: Vectors u and v emanate from the origin; w = u + alpha*v bisects the angle between them when alpha is chosen so w has equal angular distance from u and v.

given.aim: Find alpha such that w = u + alpha*v bisects the angle between u and v, given |u| = 2|v|.

terms.connects: The unit vector in the u-direction is u/|u| = u/(2|v|).

terms.example: Reference vector for the angle bisector construction.
terms.connects: The unit vector in the v-direction is v/|v|.

terms.meaning: w_bisect = u/|u| + v/|v| — the sum of the unit vectors gives a vector that bisects the angle.
terms.example: Equal-length vectors u and v give bisector u+v (alpha=1). When lengths differ, a scaling correction is needed.
terms.connects: We rewrite u/|u| + v/|v| in the form u + alpha*v to identify alpha.

given.plan: Write the angle bisector as u/|u| + v/|v|. Express this as a scalar multiple of (u + alpha*v) by factoring out 1/|u|. Identify alpha.

steps.title: Write the angle bisector direction
steps.apply: The vector bisecting the angle between u and v has the direction of u/|u| + v/|v|.
steps.note: Any positive scalar multiple of this direction also bisects the angle.

steps.title: Substitute |u| = 2|v|
steps.note: The direction is proportional to u + 2v.

steps.title: Read off alpha
steps.apply: Since u + 2v = u + alpha*v, we have alpha = 2.
steps.note: w = u + 2v bisects the angle between u and v when |u| = 2|v|.

questions.formula_note: The angle bisector of two vectors equals the sum of their unit vectors; expressing that in the required form gives alpha = |u|/|v| = 2.

questions.question:
Let {x_1, x_2, ..., x_n} be a set of linearly independent vectors in R^n. Let the (i,j)-th element of matrix A be given by A_ij = x_i^T * x_j. Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. A is invertible.
B. 0 is a singular value of A.
C. Determinant of A is 0.
D. A*z = 0 for some non-zero z.

understand.plain: The matrix A with entries A_ij = x_i^T x_j is the Gram matrix of the vectors x_1, ..., x_n. When the vectors are linearly independent, the Gram matrix is positive definite. A positive definite matrix has all positive eigenvalues, so its determinant is positive and it is invertible.

keywords.explain: The matrix G with G_ij = x_i^T x_j formed from a set of vectors. It encodes all pairwise inner products.
keywords.example: For x_1=[1,0]^T and x_2=[0,1]^T: G = [[1,0],[0,1]] = I, which is positive definite.

keywords.explain: A symmetric matrix M such that z^T M z > 0 for all non-zero z. Equivalently, all eigenvalues are strictly positive.
keywords.example: The identity matrix is positive definite: z^T I z = |z|^2 > 0 for z != 0.

keywords.explain: A matrix is invertible iff it has no zero eigenvalue iff its determinant is non-zero iff its null space contains only the zero vector.
keywords.example: A positive definite matrix always has positive determinant and is always invertible.

understand.visual_alt: The Gram matrix of n linearly independent vectors in R^n is a positive definite n-by-n matrix with all positive eigenvalues and non-zero determinant.

given.aim: Determine the correct property of the Gram matrix A with A_ij = x_i^T x_j, where {x_1,...,x_n} is a linearly independent set in R^n.

terms.meaning: An n x n symmetric matrix whose (i,j) entry is the inner product of x_i and x_j.
terms.example: If X = [x_1 | x_2 | ... | x_n] is the n x n matrix of column vectors, then A = X^T X.
terms.connects: A = X^T X. Since {x_1,...,x_n} are linearly independent and n in number, X is an n x n matrix with full rank n.

terms.meaning: When X is n x n and invertible (full rank), X^T X is also invertible.
terms.example: For X = I (identity), X^T X = I, which is invertible.
terms.connects: The Gram matrix A is positive definite and invertible when X has full column rank.

terms.meaning: z^T A z = z^T X^T X z = |Xz|^2 >= 0, with equality only when Xz=0, i.e., z=0 (since X is invertible).
terms.example: Linear independence of columns ensures Xz = 0 implies z = 0, so A is positive definite.
terms.connects: Positive definite implies all eigenvalues > 0, so det(A) > 0 and A is invertible.

given.plan: 1. Recognize A = X^T X where X = [x_1|...|x_n]. 2. Use linear independence to show X is full rank. 3. Conclude A is positive definite and hence invertible. 4. Eliminate options B, C, D.

steps.title: Identify the Gram matrix as X^T X
steps.formula_raw: A_ij = x_i^T x_j  =>  A = X^T X
steps.apply: Let X be the n x n matrix whose j-th column is x_j. Then (X^T X)_ij = (i-th row of X^T)(j-th col of X) = x_i^T x_j = A_ij. So A = X^T X.
steps.note: This is the standard form of a Gram matrix.

steps.title: X is invertible because {x_1,...,x_n} are linearly independent
steps.formula_raw: rank(X) = n  (n linearly independent vectors in R^n form a basis)
steps.apply: An n x n matrix is invertible iff its columns are linearly independent. Given that {x_1,...,x_n} are linearly independent in R^n, X has rank n, so X is invertible.
steps.note: This is the key step: linear independence of n vectors in R^n means they form a basis.

steps.title: A is positive definite
steps.formula_raw: z^T A z = z^T X^T X z = |Xz|^2 > 0 for all z != 0
steps.apply: For any non-zero z in R^n: Xz != 0 (since X is invertible), so |Xz|^2 > 0. Thus z^T A z > 0 for all z != 0, confirming A is positive definite.
steps.note: Positive definiteness implies all eigenvalues are strictly positive.

steps.title: Conclude A is invertible; eliminate other options
steps.apply: Since A is positive definite, det(A) = product of eigenvalues > 0, so A is invertible. This rules out: B (0 is a singular value would mean A is singular — false), C (det=0 — false), D (Az=0 for non-zero z means z is in null space — null space is trivial since A is invertible — false).
steps.note: Option A is the only correct statement.

questions.formula_note: The Gram matrix of n linearly independent vectors in R^n equals X^T X with X square and invertible, making it positive definite and hence invertible.

questions.question:
Let R^n be the set of real numbers, U be a subspace of R^n, and M be the matrix corresponding to the projection onto the subspace U. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. If U is a 1-dimensional subspace of R^n, then the null space of M is a 1-dimensional subspace.
B. If U is a 2-dimensional subspace of R^n, then the null space of M is a (n-2)-dimensional subspace.
C. M^2 = M.
D. M^T = M.

understand.plain: An orthogonal projection matrix M onto a subspace U has two fundamental properties: it is idempotent (M^2 = M) and symmetric (M^T = M). The null space of M is the orthogonal complement of U (all vectors perpendicular to U), which has dimension n - dim(U). These properties allow us to evaluate each option.

keywords.explain: A matrix M such that applying it twice gives the same result as applying it once: M^2 = M. For orthogonal projection onto U, it is also symmetric: M^T = M.
keywords.example: Projection onto the x-axis in R^2: M = [[1,0],[0,0]]. Then M^2 = M and M^T = M.

keywords.explain: The null space of M is the set of vectors mapped to zero — i.e., vectors orthogonal to U (the orthogonal complement U^perp).
keywords.example: Projecting onto the x-axis: null space = {[0,y]^T | y in R} = y-axis = (x-axis)^perp.

keywords.explain: A matrix M satisfying M^2 = M. Every projection matrix is idempotent.
keywords.example: For any projection P, applying P to a vector already in U leaves it unchanged, so P^2 = P.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><line x1='30' y1='190' x2='390' y2='190' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='210' y1='30' x2='210' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='30' y1='190' x2='390' y2='60' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='250' y1='80' x2='250' y2='190' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><circle cx='250' cy='80' r='5' class='ok'/><circle cx='250' cy='190' r='5' class='no'/><text x='255' y='78' class='lbl ink'>x</text><text x='255' y='205' class='lbl no'>Mx (projection)</text><text x='33' y='58' class='lbl' style='fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>U (subspace)</text><text x='260' y='140' class='sub mut'>null component</text><text x='30' y='215' class='sub mut'>Null space of M = U^perp</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A vector x projected orthogonally onto subspace U gives Mx on U; the component dropped (x - Mx) lies in the orthogonal complement U^perp, which is the null space of M.

given.aim: Identify which properties hold for the orthogonal projection matrix M onto a subspace U of R^n.

terms.meaning: The unique matrix satisfying: for any x in R^n, Mx is the closest point in U to x.
terms.example: Projection onto a line spanned by unit vector u: M = u*u^T.
terms.connects: Such M is always symmetric (M^T=M) and idempotent (M^2=M).

terms.meaning: The set of all x with Mx=0, i.e., vectors orthogonal to U (the orthogonal complement U^perp).
terms.example: For projection onto a 2-D plane in R^5, the null space is the 3-D orthogonal complement.
terms.connects: dim(null(M)) = n - dim(U) by the rank-nullity theorem (rank(M) = dim(U)).

terms.meaning: Projecting twice is the same as projecting once, because a projected vector is already in U.
terms.example: If Mx is in U, then M(Mx) = Mx, confirming M^2 = M.
terms.connects: This is a universal property of all projection matrices.

given.plan: Evaluate each option using: null space dimension = n - dim(U); M^2 = M (idempotence); M^T = M (symmetry of orthogonal projection).

steps.title: Option C: M^2 = M (idempotence)
steps.apply: For any x in R^n, Mx lies in U. Projecting a vector already in U onto U leaves it unchanged, so M(Mx) = Mx. Therefore M^2 = M. Option C is TRUE.
steps.note: This is the defining property of any projection matrix.

steps.title: Option D: M^T = M (symmetry)
steps.apply: The orthogonal projection matrix onto U is given by M = X(X^T X)^(-1) X^T (when X is a matrix whose columns span U). This is visibly symmetric. Equivalently, the orthogonality condition (x - Mx) perpendicular to U for all x forces M^T = M. Option D is TRUE.
steps.note: This holds for orthogonal projection; an oblique projection need not be symmetric.

steps.title: Option B: Null space of M when dim(U)=2
steps.formula_raw: dim(null(M)) = n - rank(M) = n - dim(U) = n - 2
steps.apply: rank(M) = dim(U) = 2 (M maps onto U). By the rank-nullity theorem: dim(null(M)) = n - 2. So the null space is (n-2)-dimensional. Option B is TRUE.
steps.note: The null space of M is exactly U^perp, the orthogonal complement of U.

steps.title: Option A: Null space of M when dim(U)=1
steps.apply: When dim(U)=1, dim(null(M)) = n - 1. Option A claims the null space is 1-dimensional. This is FALSE unless n=2. For general n >= 2, the null space has dimension n-1, not 1.
steps.note: Option A confuses the dimension of U with the dimension of its complement.

questions.formula_note: The null space of a projection onto U has dimension n - dim(U) by rank-nullity; the symmetric and idempotent properties M^T=M and M^2=M hold for every orthogonal projection.
